narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 32, you've worked hard to build your profile for law school, coming from a lower-middle income family and planning to attend full-time like most applicants. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 40.7% chance of failure for you—high enough that it tipped the decision toward rejecting your application to help ensure we admit students most likely to succeed on the bar. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is much lower than the typical student's rank of around the 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model that your later academic performance raised concerns about bar exam readiness. On a more positive note, your family income bracket actually worked in your favor, pushing the prediction toward passing compared to higher-income peers who sometimes face different pressures. Your undergraduate GPA also helped, as it's right in line with the average and contributed positively to showing you have the foundational strength to pass. Even your Year 1 decile of 3, your LSAT score, and full-time status nudged things slightly toward success, though not enough to overcome the Year 3 concern. Compared to typical students—who often have stronger Year 3 rankings around the 5th decile, higher LSATs near 37, and similar GPAs—your profile stands out for that later dip in school performance, which the model weighed most heavily. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest background; it's just the model's data-driven read on bar passage patterns. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year deciles, perhaps prepping more intensively for the LSAT, or gaining experience that strengthens your academic trajectory. Many students in similar situations improve their profiles and succeed on reapplication—reach out to advisors for targeted strategies. You've got a strong GPA foundation; building on that could shift your odds dramatically next time. We're rooting for your success.
